<!-- Generated by `dsd explain --markdown`. Do not edit by hand. -->

# DashDiag checks reference

Every health check `dsd` runs, with what it looks at, why it matters, how the
verdict is decided, and how to investigate and fix it. Run `dsd explain <topic>`
for any of these, or `dsd explain --search <keyword>` to find one by content.

- [BIND / named DNS server](#bind--named-dns-server)

## BIND / named DNS server (`bind`)

> Authoritative/recursive DNS server health.

- **What it checks:** named service state, config validity (named-checkconf, includes followed), per-zone validity (named-checkzone), port 53 TCP+UDP, a live query test, and rndc status.
- **Why it matters:** If this host serves DNS, named being down or holding a broken zone breaks name resolution for everything that points at it — a wide blast radius from a single config typo.
- **How dsd decides:** Service inactive or a config/zone error is CRIT; port 53 not listening is flagged. Gated on named actually running, so non-DNS hosts stay silent.
- **Investigate:** `named-checkconf` · `named-checkzone <zone> <file>` · `rndc status` · `dig @127.0.0.1 <name>`
- **Fix:** fix the config/zone error the check named, then reload: rndc reload; ensure named is enabled and listening on 53
- *Aliases:* named, dns-server

## Network bonding (`bonding`)

> Link-aggregation bond and slave-interface health.

- **What it checks:** Bond interfaces and their slave links — up/down state, the active slave, and mode (active-backup, 802.3ad/LACP, …).
- **Why it matters:** A bond exists for redundancy or throughput; a silently-down slave means you've already lost half your redundancy (or bandwidth) and one more failure takes the link down entirely — invisible until it does.
- **How dsd decides:** A down or failed slave is flagged. Mode is reported so an active-backup bond with one standby slave isn't mistaken for a fault.
- **Investigate:** `cat /proc/net/bonding/bond0` · `ip -br link show type bond` · `ethtool <slave>`
- **Fix:** bring the down slave back (cable/switch port/driver); for LACP, confirm the switch side is configured for the same aggregation
- *Aliases:* bond, lacp, teaming

## Ceph cluster (`ceph`)

> Ceph cluster reachability and health from this node.

- **What it checks:** Whether the node is configured for a Ceph cluster (`/etc/ceph/ceph.conf`) and, if so, whether its monitors are reachable and the cluster reports healthy.
- **Why it matters:** A node that thinks it's part of a Ceph cluster but can't reach the mons has lost its storage backend — VMs and filesystems on it stall. Silence here (an old bug) hid a real outage.
- **How dsd decides:** A configured-but-unreachable cluster is CRIT (rather than staying silent). HEALTH_WARN/HEALTH_ERR from a reachable cluster is surfaced.
- **Investigate:** `ceph -s` · `ceph health detail` · `ceph mon stat`
- **Fix:** restore mon connectivity (network/mon daemons); address the specific HEALTH_WARN/ERR (OSDs down, PGs degraded, …)
- *Aliases:* rbd, cephfs

## Time synchronization (`clock`)

> Whether the system clock is disciplined by NTP.

- **What it checks:** Synchronization state from timedatectl/chrony, and whether the RTC is in local time. Drift and sync source are reported.
- **Why it matters:** Clock skew breaks TLS validation, Kerberos/AD auth, distributed databases, log correlation, and certificate checks. It causes failures that look like everything-but-the-clock.
- **How dsd decides:** Not synchronized → CRIT, unless the RTC-in-local-time case (common dual-boot-with-Windows) is detected — then WARN, since NTP may actually be fine and the kernel is just reporting unsync.
- **Investigate:** `timedatectl status` · `chronyc tracking` · `systemctl status chronyd systemd-timesyncd`
- **Fix:** enable an NTP client (chrony/systemd-timesyncd); dual-boot RTC: timedatectl set-local-rtc 0
- *Aliases:* ntp, time, chrony

## containerd runtime (`containerd`)

> Standalone containerd socket, service, and container counts.

- **What it checks:** containerd socket presence, service state, version, and namespace/container counts — for hosts running containerd directly (not under Kubernetes, which is covered by the k8s check).
- **Why it matters:** If containers run on containerd directly, the daemon being down takes them all with it. Catching a crashed containerd is the difference between a quick restart and a confusing 'everything is gone'.
- **How dsd decides:** Socket present but service not active is CRIT (crashed daemon). Gated so it doesn't double-count with the Kubernetes path.
- **Investigate:** `systemctl status containerd` · `ctr version` · `ctr -n <ns> containers list`
- **Fix:** restart containerd and check its journal for the crash cause; verify the socket path and permissions
- *Aliases:* ctr, cri

## CPU load (`cpu`)

> Sustained CPU saturation relative to core count.

- **What it checks:** Load average from /proc/loadavg, normalized to the number of CPUs (load ÷ cores), plus per-core breakdown and steal/iowait in deep mode.
- **Why it matters:** Load sustained above core count means runnable work is queuing — requests wait, latency climbs. On VMs, high 'steal' means the hypervisor is starving this guest of physical CPU, which no amount of in-guest tuning fixes.
- **How dsd decides:** WARN at ~70% of capacity (load/cores), CRIT at ~90%. Brief spikes are normal; the 1-minute load average smooths them, so a verdict here means it has been busy, not momentarily blipped.
- **Investigate:** `uptime` · `top -b -n1 | head -20` · `ps aux --sort=-%cpu | head` · `mpstat -P ALL 1 3`
- **Fix:** identify the top consumer and whether it's expected; on a VM, check steal: if high, the host is oversubscribed — raise it with your provider
- *Aliases:* load, cpu-load, processor

## Cron & scheduled jobs (`cron`)

> Cron daemon health, recent job failures, and anacron staleness.

- **What it checks:** Whether a cron daemon is running, job failures in the last 24h (from the journal/syslog), crontab quality issues, and anacron staleness (jobs that should have run but didn't).
- **Why it matters:** A dead cron daemon or a silently-failing job means scheduled maintenance, backups, or cleanups stop happening — invisible until the thing they were supposed to do is missing.
- **How dsd decides:** A failed cron job in the window is surfaced; a stopped daemon or stale anacron is flagged. (systemd timers are a separate mechanism — their failed runs show under the systemd check.)
- **Investigate:** `systemctl status cron crond` · `journalctl -u cron --since '24h ago' | grep -i error` · `crontab -l` · `ls -la /var/spool/anacron`
- **Fix:** read the job's output/mail for the failure; ensure the daemon is enabled and running
- *Aliases:* crontab, anacron

## CVE / security advisories (`cve`)

> Pending security updates folded into health, escalated by CVSS and CISA KEV.

- **What it checks:** The package manager's pending security advisories (dnf/zypper expose real severity/CVSS; apt does not), cross-referenced against the local CISA Known-Exploited-Vulnerabilities catalog.
- **Why it matters:** Unpatched, actively-exploited CVEs are how most breaches start. KEV membership means it's being exploited in the wild right now — that outranks CVSS.
- **How dsd decides:** Any KEV match → CRIT. Otherwise CVSS ≥9.0 → CRIT, ≥7.0 → WARN (dnf/zypper). apt has no CVSS, so its name-inferred severities fold into a single honest WARN ('apt exposes no CVSS') rather than claiming a CVSS score it never measured.
- **Investigate:** `dsd cve --all` · `dnf updateinfo list security` · `apt list --upgradable`
- **Fix:** patch KEV and critical advisories first; dnf upgrade --security / apt upgrade the affected packages
- *Aliases:* security-updates, vulnerabilities, patches, kev

## Filesystem capacity (`disk`)

> Filesystem space and inode exhaustion.

- **What it checks:** Per-mount used % and inode used % from statfs, skipping network/virtual mounts. Stale NFS/CIFS mounts are detected without blocking.
- **Why it matters:** A full root filesystem breaks logging, package management, and most daemons. Inode exhaustion fills 'no space left' while df shows free bytes — a confusing outage if you only watch capacity.
- **How dsd decides:** WARN at 80% used, CRIT at 90% (same for inodes). Capacity verdicts are aligned across `dsd disk` and `dsd health` from one source so they never disagree.
- **Investigate:** `df -h` · `df -i` · `du -xhd1 / | sort -h | tail` · `find / -xdev -size +500M 2>/dev/null`
- **Fix:** clear the largest offenders (logs, caches, old kernels); for inode exhaustion, delete many small files (often a runaway temp/cache dir)
- *Aliases:* filesystem, fs, space, df

## Docker / container health (`docker`)

> Container crash loops, OOM kills, and risky daemon/container config.

- **What it checks:** Container states and exit codes, restart counts (crash loops), OOM-killed containers, daemon health and log driver, plus security posture: root user, mounted docker socket, exposed secrets, IP-forward and firewalld/nftables interaction.
- **Why it matters:** A container restart-looping is a service that's effectively down. An OOM-killed container points at a memory limit that's too low or a leak. A container running as root with the docker socket mounted is a host-takeover primitive.
- **How dsd decides:** Restart loops and OOM kills are flagged with the container named. Security findings (root + socket mount, plaintext secrets) WARN. Clean-exit (exit 0) oneshot/init containers are not counted as failures.
- **Investigate:** `docker ps -a` · `docker logs <name> --tail 50` · `docker events --since 1h` · `docker inspect <name>`
- **Fix:** crash loop: read the container's logs for the startup error; OOM: raise the memory limit or fix the leak; drop root and unmount the socket unless the container genuinely needs them
- *Aliases:* containers, compose, podman

## Physical drive health (SMART) (`drives`)

> Drive-level failure prediction from SMART, not just filesystem space.

- **What it checks:** SMART overall health, NVMe percentage-used (endurance) and available-spare, media errors, reallocated/pending sectors on SATA, and power-on hours.
- **Why it matters:** SMART predicts impending failure before data is lost. Endurance (% used) and spare capacity are the real wear signals on SSD/NVMe; reallocated sectors are the canary on spinning disks.
- **How dsd decides:** SMART self-assessment FAIL is CRIT. NVMe wear ≥90% or spare at/below the drive's threshold is WARN/CRIT. Power-on hours is INFO only (age, not wear) — a healthy old enterprise drive is fine; never read hours as a failure signal.
- **Investigate:** `smartctl -a /dev/sdX` · `nvme smart-log /dev/nvmeXn1` · `nvme list`
- **Fix:** a FAILING or high-wear drive: back up now and plan replacement; reallocated/pending sectors climbing over time = replace
- *Aliases:* smart, nvme, ssd, hdd, wear

## Kernel entropy (`entropy`)

> Available randomness for cryptographic operations.

- **What it checks:** Available entropy from /proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail and whether a hardware RNG / rngd is feeding the pool.
- **Why it matters:** Low entropy blocks any operation needing randomness — TLS handshakes, SSH key generation, even boot can stall waiting for the pool to fill. On headless VMs without a hardware RNG this is a classic 'hangs at startup' cause.
- **How dsd decides:** A persistently low entropy pool WARNs. Modern kernels (5.6+) seed from CPU instructions so this is rarer, but older kernels and minimal VMs still hit it.
- **Investigate:** `cat /proc/sys/kernel/random/entropy_avail` · `systemctl status rng-tools rngd` · `cat /sys/class/misc/hw_random/rng_current`
- **Fix:** install and enable rng-tools (rngd) or haveged; on a VM, expose a virtio-rng device from the host
- *Aliases:* random, rng

## File descriptor limits (`fdlimits`)

> Open file descriptors against the configured ceiling.

- **What it checks:** System-wide open FDs (/proc/sys/fs/file-nr) and high-usage processes against their nofile limit.
- **Why it matters:** Hitting the FD limit throws 'too many open files' — new connections and file opens fail while the process keeps running, so it manifests as partial, confusing failures rather than a clean crash. Common with connection-leaking apps and busy proxies.
- **How dsd decides:** Usage approaching the limit WARNs. A high absolute count is fine if the limit is high; it's the ratio to the ceiling that matters.
- **Investigate:** `cat /proc/sys/fs/file-nr` · `ls /proc/<pid>/fd | wc -l` · `cat /proc/<pid>/limits | grep 'open files'`
- **Fix:** raise LimitNOFILE in the unit (or /etc/security/limits.conf); fix the leak if the count only ever grows
- *Aliases:* fd, openfiles, ulimit, nofile

## Firewall (firewalld / nftables) (`firewall`)

> Firewall backend state and risky zone/forwarding config.

- **What it checks:** firewalld/nftables backend and running state, default zone, and interactions that bite containers (IP forwarding, a trusted-zone catch-all, the firewalld↔Docker nftables relationship).
- **Why it matters:** A misconfigured firewall either locks you out or silently exposes services. A trusted-zone catch-all or disabled IP forwarding is a common cause of 'the container has no network' or 'the port is open to the world' surprises.
- **How dsd decides:** Backend/state issues and risky-but-legitimate configs are surfaced. Container-relevant checks (IP forward, firewalld trusted zone) are tuned so a normal Docker host isn't false-flagged.
- **Investigate:** `firewall-cmd --state` · `firewall-cmd --get-active-zones` · `nft list ruleset` · `sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward`
- **Fix:** scope rules to the right zone; avoid a blanket trusted zone; for container networking, ensure IP forwarding and the docker chains are intact
- *Aliases:* firewalld, nftables, iptables

## GPU health (`gpu`)

> GPU temperature, power, and memory pressure across vendors.

- **What it checks:** AMD amdgpu via sysfs (temperature, TDP, VRAM use, clocks, Mesa), Intel i915 temperature, and NVIDIA via nvidia-smi as a fallback. VRAM-pressure checks are suppressed on APUs (shared memory).
- **Why it matters:** A thermally-throttling GPU silently loses performance; VRAM exhaustion causes allocation failures or OOM in compute/render workloads. On headless compute boxes nobody is watching the GPU until a job fails.
- **How dsd decides:** High temperature and VRAM pressure WARN. APU VRAM checks are gated off (an APU sharing system RAM isn't 'out of VRAM' the way a discrete card is).
- **Investigate:** `nvidia-smi` · `cat /sys/class/drm/card0/device/hwmon/hwmon*/temp1_input` · `radeontop` · `intel_gpu_top`
- **Fix:** thermal: improve airflow / check fan curves; VRAM: reduce batch size / model size, or move to a larger card
- *Aliases:* amdgpu, nvidia, vram

## SSH & host hardening (`hardening`)

> SSH daemon policy and basic account hygiene.

- **What it checks:** Effective sshd config (sshd -T): password auth, root login, weak ciphers/MACs/KEX, plus empty passwords and account expiry. Match-block overrides are not mistaken for global policy.
- **Why it matters:** SSH is the front door. Password auth and permitted root login are the two settings most often abused for brute-force entry; weak MACs/ciphers expose sessions to downgrade attacks.
- **How dsd decides:** Password auth enabled, root login permitted, or weak crypto each WARN. These are policy recommendations, not failures — a deliberately password-auth host is a choice, surfaced so it's a choice and not an oversight.
- **Investigate:** `sshd -T | grep -Ei 'passwordauth|permitroot|macs|ciphers'` · `passwd -Sa | awk '$2=="NP"'`
- **Fix:** prefer key-based auth: set PasswordAuthentication no; set PermitRootLogin prohibit-password (or no)
- *Aliases:* ssh, sshd

## Disk I/O (`io`)

> Block device saturation and latency.

- **What it checks:** Per-device utilization % and average wait, sampled from /proc/diskstats over 1 second (deep/standalone). Thresholds are drive-type aware (NVMe/SSD vs HDD).
- **Why it matters:** A saturated device backs up every process doing I/O — even healthy services stall waiting in D-state. 100% util on an NVMe is abnormal and usually points at a runaway process or btrfs maintenance.
- **How dsd decides:** Utilization and await thresholds vary by drive type (an HDD at 100% util is normal under load; an NVMe at 100% is not). 100% util adds a btrfs-cleaner note since that's a common benign-looking cause.
- **Investigate:** `iostat -x 1 5` · `iotop -ao` · `ps -eo state,pid,cmd | grep '^D'`
- **Fix:** identify the I/O hog with iotop; if btrfs, check whether btrfs-cleaner/scrub is running
- *Aliases:* iostat, diskio, disk-io

## IPMI / BMC sensors (`ipmi`)

> Baseboard management controller and hardware sensor reads.

- **What it checks:** Whether IPMI hardware is present, and the BMC/sensor reads (fans, temps, power, chassis) when it is.
- **Why it matters:** The BMC is your out-of-band window into the physical server — failing fans, a dead PSU, or a thermal event show up here before they take the host down. A BMC that can't be read is itself a problem on a server.
- **How dsd decides:** A failed BMC/sensor read on a host with IPMI hardware WARNs (rather than being swallowed). Gated on IPMI presence, so VMs/desktops stay silent.
- **Investigate:** `ipmitool sensor` · `ipmitool sel list` · `ipmitool chassis status`
- **Fix:** check the failing sensor's hardware (fan/PSU/temp); if the BMC itself is unreadable, verify the ipmi kernel modules and the BMC's own health
- *Aliases:* bmc, sensors-ipmi

## Kubernetes cluster & node health (`k8s`)

> Node conditions, problem events, stuck pods, and the OS layer under kubelet.

- **What it checks:** Node conditions (MemoryPressure/DiskPressure/PIDPressure/Ready), recent Warning events, PVC health, Deployments/StatefulSets, pods stuck Terminating, plus OS-layer: kubelet state, CNI binaries, IP forwarding, cert expiry.
- **Why it matters:** Node pressure conditions evict pods; a stuck FailedCreatePodSandBox usually means a CNI problem at the OS layer that kubectl alone won't show. Catching the OS-layer cause is what turns a vague 'pods won't schedule' into a fix.
- **How dsd decides:** Not-Ready nodes and pressure conditions escalate; recurring Warning events (e.g. FailedCreatePodSandBox) and expiring certs are surfaced. CNI checks are tuned to the detected plugin where possible.
- **Investigate:** `kubectl get nodes -o wide` · `kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail` · `kubectl get pods -A | grep -v Running` · `journalctl -u kubelet -n 50`
- **Fix:** node pressure: free the constrained resource (disk/mem) on that node; sandbox failures: check the CNI plugin and its config on the node
- *Aliases:* kubernetes, kubelet, pods

## KVM / libvirt guests (`kvm`)

> Virtual machine, network, and storage-pool health under libvirt.

- **What it checks:** VM states (running/paused/shut-off/crashed via virsh), per-disk I/O errors (domblkerror), libvirt networks, and storage-pool capacity.
- **Why it matters:** A crashed or paused guest is a workload that's down or frozen; a paused VM is often a host that ran out of disk for the image. Pool exhaustion stops new guests and snapshots. These are invisible from inside the guest.
- **How dsd decides:** Crashed → CRIT, paused → WARN, an autostart guest left down → WARN, disk I/O errors → CRIT, inactive networks → WARN, pools over ~85% → WARN.
- **Investigate:** `virsh list --all` · `virsh domblkerror <vm>` · `virsh pool-list --all` · `journalctl -u libvirtd -n 50`
- **Fix:** paused-on-ENOSPC: free space on the pool storage and `virsh resume`; check the guest's qemu log under /var/log/libvirt/qemu/
- *Aliases:* libvirt, virsh, vm

## Log severity & crashes (`logs`)

> Error/warning volume and crash artifacts from the journal and crash dirs.

- **What it checks:** ERROR and WARNING counts from the journal in the last hour, the top deduplicated error messages, and crash artifacts in /var/crash, systemd-coredump, and /sys/fs/pstore from the last 30 days.
- **Why it matters:** A sudden flood of errors is the earliest sign of a developing incident, often before a health metric crosses a threshold. A fresh coredump or pstore record is direct evidence something crashed — including a prior kernel panic.
- **How dsd decides:** High error volume WARNs; recent crash dumps are surfaced. pstore panic records are recency-gated so one old panic doesn't produce a perpetual CRIT.
- **Investigate:** `journalctl -p err --since '1h ago' | tail` · `journalctl -p err --since '1h ago' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head` · `ls -lt /var/lib/systemd/coredump/ /var/crash/`
- **Fix:** triage the most frequent error first; investigate any fresh coredump/pstore record for the crashing component
- *Aliases:* journal, journald, coredump, crash

## LVM volumes & thin pools (`lvm`)

> Volume-group space and thin-pool data/metadata exhaustion.

- **What it checks:** VG free space, thin-pool data and metadata usage, snapshot fill, and LVM RAID/mirror sync state.
- **Why it matters:** A thin pool that hits 100% data OR metadata is a hard stop — writes to every thin volume on it fail or the pool goes read-only, which can corrupt filesystems. Metadata exhaustion is the sneaky one: tiny by default, easy to overlook.
- **How dsd decides:** Thin-pool data/metadata over the warning threshold WARNs, near-full CRITs. A classic snapshot filling toward its origin size is flagged before it drops.
- **Investigate:** `lvs -o +data_percent,metadata_percent` · `vgs` · `lvs -a -o +devices,lv_health_status`
- **Fix:** extend the thin pool: lvextend --poolmetadatasize / -L +Ng; delete stale snapshots; monitor data%/metadata% going forward
- *Aliases:* lvs, vgs, thinpool

## Memory usage (`memory`)

> Real memory pressure (not just 'used' — Linux uses free RAM for cache).

- **What it checks:** Used percentage from /proc/meminfo (MemTotal vs MemAvailable), slab size, and overcommit mode. 'Available' is what apps can actually get back, so cache doesn't count as pressure.
- **Why it matters:** True memory exhaustion triggers the OOM killer, which terminates processes unpredictably. Reading 'used' naively (counting cache) cries wolf; reading MemAvailable reflects real pressure.
- **How dsd decides:** WARN at 80% of MemAvailable consumed, CRIT at 95%. Overcommit is only flagged CRIT under strict accounting (vm.overcommit_memory=2), since the default heuristic mode legitimately overcommits.
- **Investigate:** `free -h` · `cat /proc/meminfo` · `ps aux --sort=-%mem | head` · `dmesg | grep -i oom`
- **Fix:** find the consumer; cap it with a cgroup/MemoryMax if it's a service; add swap as a safety margin (not a substitute for RAM)
- *Aliases:* mem, ram

## Network reachability & TCP health (`network`)

> Interface state, gateway/internet/DNS reachability, and TCP kernel counters.

- **What it checks:** Primary interface link, gateway and internet ping (TCP-fallback when ICMP is blocked), DNS resolution time, socket states (TIME_WAIT/CLOSE_WAIT), and TcpExt counters (SYN retransmits, listen overflows, retransmit failures).
- **Why it matters:** Reachability failures are the fastest path to a user-visible outage. CLOSE_WAIT buildup signals a connection leak in an app; listen-queue overflows mean a service's accept backlog is saturated and connections are dropped.
- **How dsd decides:** Down primary / total unreachability → CRIT; gateway-silent-but-internet-up → INFO (common with probe-blocking routers). The TcpExt counters are cumulative since boot, so they're rate-normalized against uptime — one old spike on a long-lived host is INFO, not a live alarm.
- **Investigate:** `ip -br addr` · `ss -s` · `ss -tan state close-wait` · `cat /proc/net/netstat | grep TcpExt`
- **Fix:** CLOSE_WAIT leak: find the app not closing sockets; listen overflows: raise net.core.somaxconn and the app's backlog
- *Aliases:* net, tcp, gateway, dns

## NFS mounts (`nfs`)

> NFS reachability, stale handles, and risky mount options.

- **What it checks:** Server reachability (TCP probe, no ICMP needed), non-blocking stale-mount detection, and mount-option audit (soft without timeo, nolock, old vers, missing _netdev).
- **Why it matters:** A stale or hung NFS mount drags the whole host down — any process touching it wedges in uninterruptible D-state and can't even be killed. A `soft` mount without a timeout, or a missing `_netdev`, turns a transient blip into a boot hang.
- **How dsd decides:** An unreachable server or stale mount is flagged (detected without blocking the scan). Risky mount options WARN. Detection uses a 2-second timeout so a dead mount never hangs dsd itself.
- **Investigate:** `mount -t nfs,nfs4` · `showmount -e <server>` · `nfsstat -m` · `cat /proc/mounts | grep nfs`
- **Fix:** recover or unmount the dead export (lazy: umount -l); fix fstab options: add _netdev, a sane timeo, and reconsider soft mounts
- *Aliases:* nfsmount, mount

## OOM-killer events (`oom`)

> Processes terminated by the kernel out-of-memory killer.

- **What it checks:** Recent OOM-kill events from the kernel log / journal, the victim process, and (in deep mode) the cgroup/container scope they happened in.
- **Why it matters:** An OOM kill is the kernel forcibly terminating a process to free memory — abrupt, with no clean shutdown, so it shows up as a mysterious crash or vanished service. The victim is often not the actual memory hog, just the biggest one at the wrong moment.
- **How dsd decides:** A recent OOM kill is surfaced (CRIT near the event, decaying with age so an hours-old kill doesn't pin the verdict). Deep mode attributes it to a cgroup/container so you know whose limit was hit.
- **Investigate:** `dmesg -T | grep -i 'killed process'` · `journalctl -k | grep -i oom` · `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/<slice>/memory.events`
- **Fix:** raise the limit (MemoryMax/container memory) or fix the leak in the real hog; add swap as a buffer; check for a runaway allocation
- *Aliases:* oomkill, out-of-memory, killed

## Process anomalies (`processes`)

> Zombie processes, uninterruptible-sleep (D-state), and process count.

- **What it checks:** Total process count, zombies (defunct children a parent never reaped), and processes stuck in D-state (uninterruptible sleep — usually blocked on I/O).
- **Why it matters:** A pile of zombies signals a buggy parent leaking process slots — eventually the PID table fills. A D-state process can't even be killed and usually means a stuck device or hung network mount dragging the system down.
- **How dsd decides:** Growing zombie counts and processes wedged in D-state are surfaced. A single transient D-state during heavy I/O is normal; persistent ones are not.
- **Investigate:** `ps -eo state,pid,ppid,cmd | grep '^Z'` · `ps -eo state,pid,cmd | grep '^D'` · `cat /proc/<pid>/stack`
- **Fix:** zombies: fix or restart the parent that isn't reaping; D-state: trace the blocking resource (often a stale NFS mount or failing disk)
- *Aliases:* procs, zombie, dstate

## Proxmox VE node (`pve`)

> Proxmox node health — tasks, backups, bridges, quorum.

- **What it checks:** Node/VM/CT status, recent task errors, per-guest backup recency (not just a node-wide age), network bridge health, and cluster quorum.
- **Why it matters:** On a Proxmox host the VMs are the workload. A failed backup task or a guest silently missing from the backup job is invisible until you need to restore and can't. Lost quorum freezes the whole cluster's ability to make changes.
- **How dsd decides:** Task errors and lost quorum escalate. Backup audit is per-guest, so one guest missing from the job is flagged even when the node-wide 'last backup' looks recent.
- **Investigate:** `pvesh get /cluster/tasks` · `pvesh get /nodes/<node>/status` · `pvecm status` · `cat /etc/pve/jobs.cfg`
- **Fix:** investigate the failing task in the GUI/journal; add the missing guest to the backup job; restore quorum before making changes
- *Aliases:* proxmox, pvenode

## Software RAID (mdadm) (`raid`)

> mdadm array state — degraded, recovering, or failed.

- **What it checks:** Array state from /proc/mdstat and mdadm — clean / degraded / recovering / failed, and failed/spare member devices.
- **Why it matters:** A degraded array has lost its redundancy — one more disk failure means data loss, and you're running on borrowed time until the rebuild finishes. A silently-failed member is a flat tire you didn't notice.
- **How dsd decides:** Degraded or failed arrays → CRIT; an active resync/recovery is surfaced (informational while it runs). A faulty member device is named.
- **Investigate:** `cat /proc/mdstat` · `mdadm --detail /dev/mdX` · `mdadm --examine /dev/sdX`
- **Fix:** replace the failed disk and add it: mdadm --add /dev/mdX /dev/sdY; let the rebuild finish before touching anything else
- *Aliases:* mdadm, md, mirror

## SELinux / AppArmor denials (`selinux`)

> Mandatory-access-control denials that silently break services.

- **What it checks:** Recent SELinux AVC denials (grouped by source), SELinux booleans that would resolve them, AppArmor profiles in complain/enforce mode, and the /.autorelabel flag.
- **Why it matters:** An AVC denial is the invisible reason a service 'just won't work' despite correct config — SELinux blocked it and the app only sees a generic permission error. The right fix is usually a boolean or a context, not `setenforce 0`.
- **How dsd decides:** Denials are grouped and surfaced with the boolean-first fix order (toggling a boolean is safer than writing policy). AppArmor complain-mode profiles are reported as not actually enforcing.
- **Investigate:** `ausearch -m avc -ts recent` · `sealert -a /var/log/audit/audit.log` · `getsebool -a | grep <service>` · `aa-status`
- **Fix:** prefer a boolean: setsebool -P <bool> on; fix file contexts: restorecon -Rv <path>; avoid disabling SELinux outright
- *Aliases:* avc, apparmor, mac, denials, kernelsec

## Swap usage & activity (`swap`)

> Swap fill level and live paging rate — distinguishing disk thrash from zram.

- **What it checks:** Swap used % from /proc/swaps, and the live page-in/page-out rate sampled over 1 second from /proc/vmstat (pswpin/pswpout). zram devices are detected separately.
- **Why it matters:** Sustained paging to a disk-backed swap means RAM is exhausted and every faulted page costs a disk round-trip — the classic latency cliff. Paging to zram is compressed RAM at RAM speed, which is normal and cheap.
- **How dsd decides:** Usage WARN at 20%, CRIT at 60%. Activity: below 50 pages/s is background churn (silent); above that, disk-backed swap WARNs and >100 pages/s CRITs, while zram-backed paging is reported INFO ('compressed RAM, not disk thrash').
- **Investigate:** `free -h` · `swapon --show` · `zramctl` · `vmstat 1 5`
- **Fix:** if disk swap is thrashing, the real fix is more RAM or less working set; tune vm.swappiness for the workload; consider zram for memory-constrained hosts
- *Aliases:* paging, zram

## Kernel parameters (sysctl) (`sysctl`)

> Key kernel tunables that are at values likely to bite under load.

- **What it checks:** A curated set of network and VM sysctls (e.g. net.core.somaxconn, tcp_tw_reuse, vm.swappiness, vm.dirty_ratio) compared against values appropriate for a server.
- **Why it matters:** Stock kernel defaults are tuned for a desktop, not a busy server. A small somaxconn caps the accept backlog; a high swappiness pages out hot memory. These don't show up until traffic does, then they look like mysterious latency.
- **How dsd decides:** Out-of-recommendation values are surfaced. A value sitting at the version-stable kernel default is treated as 'nobody tuned this' (informational) rather than asserting a fix was attempted and lost.
- **Investigate:** `sysctl net.core.somaxconn vm.swappiness` · `sysctl -a | grep -E 'somaxconn|tw_reuse|swappiness'`
- **Fix:** set the value and persist it in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf, then sysctl --system; match the tunable to the workload — don't cargo-cult
- *Aliases:* kernel-params, tuning

## systemd unit health (`systemd`)

> Failed units and slow-boot offenders.

- **What it checks:** Units in the failed state (systemctl --failed), slow boot units from systemd-analyze, and journal health. Gated on systemd actually being the init system.
- **Why it matters:** A failed unit is a service that isn't doing its job — and often the root cause behind a higher-level symptom. Catching it by name points straight at the problem.
- **How dsd decides:** Any failed unit is surfaced (CRIT for the failure). DBus/journald checks are gated on systemd so non-systemd hosts (Alpine/OpenRC) don't get phantom failures.
- **Investigate:** `systemctl --failed` · `systemctl status <unit>` · `journalctl -u <unit> -n 50` · `systemd-analyze blame | head`
- **Fix:** read the unit's journal for the actual error; after fixing, systemctl reset-failed <unit>
- *Aliases:* units, services, failed

## CPU / system thermals (`thermal`)

> Temperature and thermal throttling of CPU/system sensors.

- **What it checks:** Thermal-zone temperatures (/sys/class/thermal) and CPU package sensors, plus evidence of thermal throttling.
- **Why it matters:** A throttling CPU silently runs slower to avoid overheating — you pay in latency, not in a crash, so it hides. Sustained high temps also shorten hardware life. Dense servers and dusty/failing fans are the usual culprits.
- **How dsd decides:** High sensor temperatures WARN/CRIT; active throttling is surfaced. Thresholds account for the sensor type (a CPU package runs hotter than ambient).
- **Investigate:** `sensors` · `cat /sys/class/thermal/thermal_zone*/temp` · `dmesg | grep -i thermal`
- **Fix:** check airflow and clean/replace fans; verify the cooler is seated and thermal paste isn't dried out
- *Aliases:* temperature, throttle, cooling

## TLS certificate expiry (`tls`)

> Local cert files and remote endpoints approaching expiry.

- **What it checks:** Local certificate files (scanned from common paths) and, on request, remote endpoints (host:port) — reading the actual expiry even from an already-expired cert.
- **Why it matters:** An expired certificate is a hard outage: TLS handshakes fail and every client is locked out at once, usually at the worst possible time. Expiry is fully predictable, so there is no excuse to be surprised by it.
- **How dsd decides:** Certificates within the warning window WARN; already-expired or expiring-very-soon CRIT. Both file and endpoint scans report days remaining.
- **Investigate:** `dsd tls --endpoint host:443` · `openssl x509 -enddate -noout -in cert.pem` · `echo | openssl s_client -connect host:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -enddate -noout`
- **Fix:** renew and deploy before expiry (automate with certbot/ACME); reload the service after replacing the cert
- *Aliases:* certs, ssl, x509

## ZFS pool health (`zfs`)

> Pool state, scrub results, and vdev error counters.

- **What it checks:** Pool State (ONLINE/DEGRADED/FAULTED), capacity, fragmentation, scrub age, cumulative read/write/checksum vdev error counters, and unrepairable errors from the last scrub.
- **Why it matters:** A DEGRADED pool has lost redundancy — one more failure means data loss. Checksum errors caught and repaired indicate failing hardware (disk/RAM/cable) even when data is currently intact.
- **How dsd decides:** Not ONLINE → CRIT. Unrepairable scrub errors → CRIT (real corruption). The cumulative R/W/Cksum counters persist until `zpool clear` and include already-repaired errors, so on a healthy ONLINE pool with a clean scrub they are WARN ('repaired; investigate if recurring'), not a perpetual CRIT.
- **Investigate:** `zpool status -v` · `zpool list` · `zpool events`
- **Fix:** DEGRADED: replace the faulted disk and resilver; after fixing hardware and a clean scrub: zpool clear <pool> to reset counters
- *Aliases:* zpool, pool
